title: Overpainting marbled sheets
subtitle: Writing, printing, and watercolour work on a marbled ground
excerpt: A marbled sheet provides a coloured, patterned ground for calligraphy, printmaking, or watercolour work. The marbled pattern reads through the overpainted layer when transparent media are used. The principal technical challenge is that the alum mordant used in marbling reduces the surface absorbency, so inks and paints behave differently than on plain paper.
sourceNotes: Overpainting on marbled sheets is synthesised from calligraphy and watercolour practice. The alum mordant effect on absorbency is an established property noted in marbling and calligraphy teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: The effect of the alum mordant on ink and paint behaviour. Alum-sized paper accepts watercolour differently from unsized paper: colours may bead or resist slightly on the surface before settling.

content.text: Marbled paper was used historically as a ground for calligraphy, particularly in the Safavid and Ottoman court traditions, where poems and texts were written in gold or black on marbled endpapers. The contrast between the fluid pattern and the precise letterforms is the point. Modern use of marbled sheets as a ground follows the same principle.
Alum-mordanted paper accepts pigment during marbling because the alum holds the paint to the fibres. The same property causes

when overpainting: iron-gall ink and dilute watercolour may bead slightly rather than absorbing at once. Allow a few seconds after applying ink before touching the wet line. Test ink behaviour on a corner of the sheet before writing on the main area.

A Mitchell Roundhand nib 3 loaded with iron-gall ink is the most direct approach. Write at normal speed; do not slow down so much that ink pools at the end of a stroke. If the ink sits proud of the surface for more than a few seconds, dilute it very slightly with water. Gouache (white, gold, or coloured) works well on marbled grounds and is less affected by the alum mordant than iron-gall ink; it sits on the surface rather than being absorbed and produces a bold, opaque line.

Transparent watercolour washes work well on marbled grounds when applied with a loaded brush in one pass. If you work a wash back and forth repeatedly, the brush will lift the dried marbled pattern. Lay the colour in one direction and leave it. The marbled pattern shows through a dilute wash, which produces a layered, complex surface.

For calligraphy, a marbled sheet with a low-contrast colour palette lets the letterforms read clearly. A sheet with strong, high-contrast colours works better as a frame or endpaper than as a writing surface: the eye goes to the colour, not the text. For watercolour overpaint, a light-toned marble gives the most room. Deep navy or black marbling absorbs colour into darkness without adding to the image.
